unit.name: Attribution methods, hypothesis testing, and the limits of saliency maps
unit.description: Explains saliency-style explanations, then argues theoretically and empirically that popular attribution methods often cannot support behavioral conclusions about a model.
unit.summary: The lecture first defines a saliency map as a feature-wise estimate of how prediction changes locally around an input, using ImageNet pixels as the intuitive example [ts=602s] [ts=623s]. Kim then gives several reasons a gap can arise between what machines know and what humans think they know, and drills into attribution methods by showing that trained and untrained networks can produce nearly identical saliency maps, while inserted bugs such as label noise, spurious correlations, or OOD shifts are not reliably surfaced by explanations [ts=671s] [ts=743s] [ts=789s]. She reformulates the user’s interpretation problem as hypothesis testing and argues that IG and SHAP cannot validate such hypotheses better than random in theory and around random in practical end tasks like recourse and spurious-correlation detection [ts=935s] [ts=965s] [ts=1074s]. Her practical replacement is simple local sampling around the point of interest, with usefulness governed by sample count, output resolution, and feature count, plus careful validation before deploying these methods in high-stakes settings [ts=1115s] [ts=1147s] [ts=1240s].

key_points.text: A saliency map assigns each feature a number intended to reflect the local shape of the prediction function around that feature.
Trained and untrained networks can yield very similar saliency maps, so meaningful and random predictions may receive nearly indistinguishable explanations.
Inserted model problems such as labeling errors, spurious correlations, and out-of-distribution shifts are not reliably diagnosed by these explanations.
IG and SHAP are framed as hypothesis-testing tools, but the lecture argues they cannot validate user hypotheses better than random guessing.
For local function-shape questions, a direct sampling strategy around the input is recommended over complex attribution heuristics.
The result is said to apply to any model that can be treated as a function, not only vision models.

She reformulates the user’s interpretation problem as hypothesis testing and argues that IG and SHAP cannot validate such hypotheses better than random in theory and around random in practical end tasks like recourse and spurious-correlation detection [ts=935s] [ts=965s] [ts=1074s]. Her practical replacement is simple local sampling around the point of interest, with usefulness governed by sample count, output resolution, and feature count, plus careful validation before deploying these methods in high-stakes settings [ts=1115s] [ts=1147s] [ts=1240s].

kp_catalog.name: Limits of attribution methods for behavioral inference
kp_catalog.description: Saliency, IG, and SHAP may look plausible but often do not justify conclusions about what a model actually uses or how it will behave.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because limits of attribution methods for behavioral inference formalizes how saliency, IG, and SHAP may look plausible but often do not justify conclusions about what a model actually uses or how it will behave, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Hypothesis-testing view of local explanation and sampling
kp_catalog.description: Interpreting attribution can be cast as hypothesis testing, and direct local sampling offers a more reliable way to estimate function shape.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because hypothesis-testing view of local explanation and sampling clarifies interpreting attribution can be cast as hypothesis testing, and direct local sampling offers a more reliable way to estimate function shape, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
